text:
Silver-copper (AgCu) bimetallic catalysts are currently one of the most promising materials for the electrochemical carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2RR) in terms of achieving a carbon neutral future. With the vast number of designs being proposed for silver-copper catalysts, very little is known about the structural and chemical changes that these materials undergo during operation, and thus their dynamic stability. As a result, there is limited understanding of the actual active sites, and thus, optimization of catalysts for CO2RR is not being approached in a holistic manner.

Phase-blended alloyed vs. phase-separated AgCu nanoparticles were synthesized on carbon paper electrodes and their time-evolution during CO2RR was probed. Through time-sequential scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) and elemental mapping it is shown that Cu has a high mobility during the catalytic process. Specifically, Cu atoms are continuously ‘eroded’ from the bulk of the NPs, migrate to the surface and are subsequently fully depleted to form Cu-enriched, dispersed nanoparticles. The as-synthesized phase-blended NP clusters as well as NP clusters formed by phase-separation at the catalytic sites, regardless of initial phase configuration, all follow a similar evolutionary path to produce distinct Cu and Ag domains. These domains converge at values close to the thermodynamic solubility limits for the Ag0.88Cu0.12 and Ag0.05Cu0.95 alloy compositions.

Seeded electrolysis has been employed to tailor the architecture and composition of AgCu catalysts in CO2RR, facilitating the bulk and surface separation of two immiscible phases. The significance of the Ag–Cu phase boundaries towards catalytic function is therefore exemplified. Operando HERFD-XAS revealed the metallic nature of copper towards electrolysis, pinpointing the catalytic active component. Herein, this study reveals in depth the individual transformation processes taking place in AgCu bimetallic catalysts that underpin their overall activity and stability in the Nation’s most strategic low carbon energy sector.
